join_order.notes: customer bitset indexed by c_custkey (dense 1..1500000, fits in ~188KB bitset); probe orders rows that pass zone-map + date filter
build compact hash map keyed by o_orderkey from ~1.44M matched orders; probe ~31M qualifying lineitem rows with bloom filter pre-check

logical_plan.aggregation_notes: GROUP BY (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority). Estimated distinct groups ~1.44M (upper bound = matched orders). High cardinality → thread-local hash tables + final merge. Key is l_orderkey (int32); store (revenue_sum, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) per group.

filter_pushdown.encoding: dictionary int16_t — resolve 'BUILDING' to dict code at startup, compare codes

filter_pushdown.index: orders_o_orderdate zone_map — skip blocks with min > 9204
lineitem sort order is l_shipdate — zone_map skip early blocks with max <= 9204

topk.notes: Each thread maintains a min-heap of size 10 keyed by (revenue DESC, o_orderdate ASC). After parallel aggregation, merge up to 64 local heaps (640 elements) with std::partial_sort to extract global top 10.

data_structures.customer_filter: bitset(1500001 bits, ~188KB) — dense integer domain c_custkey in [1,1500000]; set bit at c_custkey for each matching 'BUILDING' customer; O(1) probe via bit test; fits comfortably in L3 cache (44MB)
data_structures.orders_join_build: compact_open_addressing_hash_map pre_sized(1440000 * 4/3 = ~1920000 slots, power-of-2) keyed by o_orderkey (int32_t), payload: {o_orderdate int32_t, o_shippriority int32_t}; Robin Hood or linear probing; ~46MB — fits in L3
data_structures.orders_bloom_filter: BloomFilter(1440000 keys, 10 bits/key = ~1.8MB, fits L3) on o_orderkey — pre-filter lineitem probe to skip ~70-80% of non-matching l_orderkey values before hash table lookup
data_structures.aggregation: thread_local_compact_hash_map per thread, keyed by l_orderkey (int32_t), payload: {revenue_sum int64_t, o_orderdate int32_t, o_shippriority int32_t}; pre-sized at estimated_groups/num_threads; final sequential merge into global result map
data_structures.topk_heap: thread_local std::priority_queue<AggResult, size=10> (min-heap by revenue then o_orderdate); merge 64 local heaps with std::partial_sort

parallelism.morsel_size_rationale: L3_cache(44MB) / 64_threads / ~4_columns_active ≈ 172KB per morsel; round down to 65536 rows for alignment

phases.rationale: 1.5M rows, single column (c_mktsegment int16_t), fast; bitset writes are non-atomic (single-threaded build avoids false sharing); takes ~2ms

phases.synchronization: thread_local_row_buffers — each thread collects matched order rows locally, then inserts into shared hash map with atomic CAS or partition-by-hash-of-orderkey to avoid contention
phases.preferred: partition_parallel: hash o_orderkey mod 64 → each thread owns 1/64 of hash map partitions; no locking needed

phases.synchronization: thread_local_aggregation_hash_maps + thread_local_topk_heaps; no shared state during hot loop
phases.notes: Bloom filter check on l_orderkey before hash map probe; eliminates ~70% of lineitem rows that have no matching order

phases.estimated_entries: ~1.44M total across all threads; sequential merge takes ~20-50ms

phases.notes: Collect up to 64*10=640 local top-10 candidates; std::partial_sort to extract global top 10

index_usage.purpose: skip_blocks_where_min > 9204 (1995-03-15 in days_since_epoch); orders are sorted by o_orderdate so later blocks (date >= 1995-03-15) can be entirely skipped; expected to eliminate ~52% of orders blocks
lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate; zone maps on l_shipdate blocks allow skipping early blocks where max <= 9204; expected to skip ~48% of lineitem blocks with dates before cutoff
index_usage.notes: No explicit zone map index file listed for lineitem l_shipdate — use sort-order-aware block skipping by reading first value of each block

index_not_used.reason: Would require random access per o_orderkey (1.44M lookups into 60M row index); scanning lineitem sequentially with filter+bloom+probe is faster on HDD due to sequential I/O vs random I/O; sequential scan at 64 threads is ~2s, random index lookups would be much worse on HDD
Customer bitset probe during orders sequential scan is O(1) per row; no benefit from index when we already scan all (date-filtered) orders blocks sequentially

scan_details.customer: single_pass: scan c_mktsegment (int16_t dict code), filter == BUILDING_CODE, read c_custkey, set bit in bitset; fused in one loop
scan_details.orders: zone_map_pruned_single_pass: skip blocks with o_orderdate zone_map min > 9204; for qualifying blocks, fused loop: check o_orderdate < 9204, probe customer bitset on o_custkey, collect (o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) into hash map
scan_details.lineitem: zone_map_aware_single_pass: skip early blocks where l_shipdate zone max <= 9204 (sort-order exploitation); for qualifying blocks, fused loop: check l_shipdate > 9204, bloom filter on l_orderkey, probe orders hash map, accumulate revenue into thread-local aggregation hash map

physical_plan.memory_pattern_notes: All three table scans are sequential column reads — optimal for HDD prefetch. Hash map probes introduce random access but are bounded by L3 cache (orders hash map ~46MB ≈ L3 44MB, borderline — consider partitioning if cache thrash observed). Bitset (188KB) is fully L2/L3 resident.
physical_plan.date_encoding: days_since_epoch int32_t; 1995-03-15 = (1995-1970)*365 + leap_days + day_of_year = 9204; compare directly as integers, no conversion needed at runtime
physical_plan.revenue_computation: l_extendedprice * (100 - l_discount) / 100 in scaled int64_t arithmetic (scale_factor=100 for both columns); final output divide by 10000 for correct decimal representation; avoids floating-point precision issues

estimated_cost.target_comparison: DuckDB=79ms, Umbra=80ms; target competitive with top systems at ~80-100ms with 64 threads and full parallelism
estimated_cost.bottleneck_risk: orders hash map (~46MB) slightly exceeds L3 cache (44MB) — if cache thrash is observed, switch to partitioned hash map (16 partitions) to keep each partition in L3
